title: Cyber Security (Notification of Cyber Security Incident) Regulations 2024
auditNote: Penalty sub-section in noi-r11 references s26(5) while penalties/index.json references s26(3) for the same conduct — exact sub-section requires gazette verification. Daily continuing fine conflict: this regulation states RM10,000 daily; penalties/index.json states RM5,000 daily — must be verified against official text.
subject: Prescribes incident notification procedures, timelines, and required information for national critical information infrastructure entities reporting cyber security incidents to the Chief Executive of NACSA

provisions.title: Citation and Commencement
provisions.requirement: These regulations may be cited as the Cyber Security (Notification of Cyber Security Incident) Regulations 2024 and shall come into operation on 26 August 2024.
provisions.explanation: Establishes the formal name of these regulations and confirms they took effect on 26 August 2024, the same date the parent Act came into force.

provisions.title: Interpretation
provisions.requirement: In these Regulations, unless the context otherwise requires: 'cyber security incident' has the meaning assigned to it in section 2 of the Act; 'initial notification' means the first notification submitted under regulation 4; 'follow-up notification' means a subsequent notification submitted under regulation 5; 'NACSA portal' means the electronic system designated by the Chief Executive for the submission of incident notifications; 'significant impact' means an impact that meets any of the criteria specified in regulation 3.
provisions.explanation: Defines key terms used throughout the regulations. Importantly, it cross-references the Act's definition of 'cyber security incident' and introduces the concept of 'significant impact' which is the threshold that triggers the mandatory 6-hour notification window.

provisions.title: Definition of Significant Impact
provisions.requirement: A cyber security incident shall be deemed to have a significant impact if it results in or is likely to result in any of the following: (a) disruption to the continuous functioning of the national critical information infrastructure for a period exceeding four hours; (b) compromise of the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of data stored in, processed by, or transmitted through the national critical information infrastructure that affects or is likely to affect national security, public safety, or essential services; (c) unauthorised access to or control of any system, network, or device forming part of the national critical information infrastructure; (d) deployment or execution of ransomware, destructive malware, or any malicious code designed to render the national critical information infrastructure inoperable; (e) compromise of any authentication system, cryptographic key, digital certificate, or access control mechanism protecting the national critical information infrastructure; (f) any incident that affects or is likely to affect more than ten thousand individuals whose personal data is stored in the national critical information infrastructure; (g) any incident that the entity reasonably believes may spread to or affect other national critical information infrastructure entities.
provisions.explanation: This is the critical threshold provision. If a cyber security incident meets ANY ONE of these seven criteria, the 6-hour mandatory notification window under regulation 4 is triggered. The criteria are deliberately broad, covering service disruption (4+ hours), data compromise affecting national interests, unauthorised access, malware deployment, authentication compromise, large-scale personal data impact (10,000+ individuals), and cross-entity contagion risk. Entities must assess incoming incidents against all seven criteria without delay.

provisions.title: Initial Notification Requirements
provisions.requirement: Where a national critical information infrastructure entity becomes aware of a cyber security incident that has or may have a significant impact, the entity shall submit an initial notification to the Chief Executive within six hours of becoming aware of the incident. The initial notification shall contain the following information: (a) the name and registration or identification number of the entity; (b) the name, designation, telephone number, and email address of the person submitting the notification and of the entity's principal point of contact for incident response; (c) the date and time the incident was detected or discovered; (d) the date and time the entity became aware that the incident has or may have a significant impact; (e) a preliminary description of the incident including the type of incident, the systems or networks affected, and the suspected or known attack vector; (f) the criteria under regulation 3 that the entity believes are met; (g) a preliminary assessment of the actual or potential impact on the entity's operations, data, and services; (h) a description of any immediate containment or mitigation measures taken or being taken; (i) whether law enforcement has been notified; (j) whether any other regulatory authority has been notified and, if so, which authority.
provisions.explanation: The 6-hour clock starts from the moment the entity 'becomes aware' of the incident meeting a significant impact criterion -- not from the time of the incident itself. The required information fields are designed to give NACSA an immediate operational picture. Entities do not need complete forensic analysis at this stage; preliminary assessments and descriptions are acceptable. However, all ten mandatory fields must be addressed even if some entries state that information is still being gathered.

provisions.title: Follow-up Notification Requirements
provisions.requirement: The entity shall submit a follow-up notification to the Chief Executive within seventy-two hours of the submission of the initial notification. The follow-up notification shall contain the following information: (a) an updated and detailed description of the incident, including the root cause if known; (b) the scope of the incident, including the number and types of systems, networks, and devices affected; (c) the volume and categories of data compromised, exfiltrated, or otherwise affected; (d) a detailed assessment of the impact on the entity's operations, services, and any third parties; (e) a comprehensive description of all containment, eradication, and recovery measures taken, in progress, or planned; (f) indicators of compromise identified, including but not limited to IP addresses, domain names, file hashes, email addresses, and malware signatures; (g) a timeline of events from initial compromise to detection and response; (h) an assessment of whether the incident has been fully contained or whether it is ongoing; (i) any additional information requested by the Chief Executive in response to the initial notification. Further follow-up notifications shall be submitted at intervals not exceeding seventy-two hours until the Chief Executive confirms in writing that no further notifications are required.
provisions.explanation: The follow-up notification must be submitted within 72 hours after the initial notification (not after the incident). It requires substantially more detail than the initial notification, reflecting the expected progress of the entity's investigation. The requirement for indicators of compromise is particularly important as it enables NACSA to alert other NCII entities about emerging threats. Follow-up notifications continue on a rolling 72-hour cycle until NACSA explicitly closes the notification requirement in writing.

provisions.title: Final Incident Report
provisions.requirement: The entity shall submit a final incident report to the Chief Executive within thirty days of the date on which the incident is fully resolved or the Chief Executive confirms that no further follow-up notifications are required, whichever is earlier. The final incident report shall contain: (a) a complete and detailed account of the incident from detection to resolution; (b) the confirmed root cause of the incident; (c) a complete inventory of all systems, data, and services affected; (d) a detailed assessment of the total impact, including financial losses, operational disruption, data loss, and reputational harm; (e) all remediation and recovery actions completed; (f) lessons learned and improvements to be implemented to prevent recurrence; (g) an updated set of indicators of compromise; (h) any information required by the Chief Executive.
provisions.explanation: The final report closes the notification lifecycle. It serves both an accountability function and a national knowledge-building function, as NACSA aggregates lessons learned across NCII sectors. The 30-day window runs from either full resolution of the incident or NACSA's written confirmation that follow-ups may cease. The report must include confirmed (not preliminary) root cause analysis and a forward-looking component on preventive improvements.

provisions.title: Notification Channels
provisions.requirement: Notifications under these Regulations shall be submitted through one or more of the following channels: (a) the NACSA portal, which shall be the primary and preferred channel for all notifications; (b) electronic mail to the designated email address published by the Chief Executive, which may be used where the NACSA portal is unavailable or inaccessible; (c) telephone to the NACSA 24-hour incident hotline, which shall be used only for the purpose of providing immediate verbal notice concurrently with or immediately prior to the submission of a written notification through channel (a) or (b). A notification submitted by telephone under paragraph (c) shall not be deemed to satisfy the notification requirement unless a corresponding written notification is submitted through channel (a) or (b) within three hours of the telephone notification.
provisions.explanation: The NACSA portal is the primary notification channel. Email is a fallback when the portal is down. Telephone notification alone is insufficient -- it must be followed by a written submission within 3 hours. This ensures that all notifications are formally documented in writing while allowing entities to give immediate verbal alerts for severe incidents.

provisions.title: Form of Notification
provisions.requirement: Every written notification under these Regulations shall be submitted in the form prescribed by the Chief Executive and published on the NACSA portal. Where an entity is unable to use the prescribed form due to the unavailability of the NACSA portal, the notification may be submitted by electronic mail in a format that substantially contains all information required under regulation 4 or 5 as applicable. The Chief Executive may from time to time amend the prescribed form by publishing an updated form on the NACSA portal, and the amended form shall take effect fourteen days after publication.
provisions.explanation: NACSA prescribes standardised forms to ensure consistency across notifications. However, the regulation pragmatically allows free-form email submissions when the portal is unavailable, provided they contain all mandatory information. Changes to forms take effect 14 days after publication, giving entities time to update their internal processes.

provisions.title: Record Keeping
provisions.requirement: Every national critical information infrastructure entity shall maintain complete and accurate records of: (a) every cyber security incident detected, regardless of whether it meets the significant impact threshold under regulation 3; (b) the assessment conducted to determine whether each incident has or may have a significant impact; (c) copies of all notifications, reports, and correspondence submitted to or received from the Chief Executive under these Regulations; (d) all internal incident response actions, decisions, and communications related to each notifiable incident; (e) all indicators of compromise, forensic evidence, and technical analysis related to each notifiable incident. Records under this regulation shall be maintained for a period of not less than seven years from the date of the incident and shall be made available to the Chief Executive or any authorised officer upon request within forty-eight hours of such request.
provisions.explanation: Record keeping extends beyond just notifiable incidents -- entities must document ALL detected incidents and the triage assessment that determined whether notification was required. This creates an audit trail that NACSA can review during inspections to verify that entities are properly identifying and escalating significant incidents. The 7-year retention period aligns with general regulatory record-keeping expectations and ensures records remain available for any subsequent investigations or legal proceedings. The 48-hour production window upon request gives entities reasonable time to compile records.

provisions.title: Confidentiality of Notifications
provisions.requirement: All information contained in notifications, reports, and correspondence submitted under these Regulations shall be treated as confidential by the Chief Executive and shall not be disclosed except: (a) to authorised officers for the purpose of carrying out their functions under the Act; (b) to other national critical information infrastructure entities to the extent necessary to prevent, detect, or respond to cyber security threats, provided that the identity of the reporting entity shall not be disclosed without the entity's prior written consent; (c) to law enforcement agencies where the Chief Executive has reasonable grounds to believe that a criminal offence has been committed; (d) as required by any written law or order of a court.
provisions.explanation: This provision addresses a common concern among reporting entities: that their incident disclosures might be used against them or leaked publicly. The regulation provides strong confidentiality protections while preserving NACSA's ability to share threat intelligence (without identifying the source) with other NCII entities. Entity identity is protected unless consent is given or law enforcement referral is necessary.

provisions.title: Penalty for Non-Compliance
provisions.requirement: Any national critical information infrastructure entity that fails to comply with regulation 4, 5, or 6 commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding ten thousand ringgit for every day during which the offence continues after conviction.
provisions.explanation: Non-compliance with notification requirements carries significant financial penalties. The RM500,000 maximum fine for a single offence and a daily continuing fine create strong incentives for timely compliance. CAUTION: Two unresolved conflicts require gazette verification before these figures can be relied upon for compliance purposes. First, the daily continuing fine stated here is RM10,000, but penalties/index.json records RM5,000 for the same offence — these figures cannot both be correct and the official gazette text must be checked. Second, this provision cites s26(5) as the enabling sub-section for this penalty but penalties/index.json cites s26(3) for notification-related offences — the correct sub-section reference must be verified. These penalties apply to the entity itself; individual liability for officers of the entity is addressed separately under the Act.
provisions.relatedActSection: s26 [sub-section reference unverified: this provision cites s26(5); penalties/index.json cites s26(3) for notification offences — verify against gazette], s60
provisions.verificationStatus: penalty-figures-unverified: daily continuing fine stated as RM10,000 here but RM5,000 in penalties/index.json — must verify against official gazette; enabling sub-section s26(5) vs s26(3) also requires verification

notes: This content is indicative and does not reproduce the official gazette text. Specific figures (e.g. fine amounts, timelines, insurance thresholds, fees) are examples based on publicly available information and may differ from the actual subsidiary legislation. Always refer to the official P.U.(A) gazette for authoritative requirements.
